answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe the type of pain and if it is worse with any specific movements or activities?
answers.intake_a2: It started when I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q3: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q4: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q5: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a5: icing the first day and it did help
answers.intake_q6: Is the pain localized to the outside (lateral) part of the elbow or somewhere else?
answers.intake_q7: Any difficulty gripping objects or performing activities that require wrist extension?
answers.intake_a7: a little difficulty lifting objects due to pain, but not gripping
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?
answers.intake_q9: Have you had any previous injuries or similar pain episodes in this elbow before?
answers.intake_q10: Have you noticed any clicking, locking, or instability in the elbow joint?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside (lateral) part of her right elbow. The pain began while playing tennis and is described as worsening with certain movements, particularly those involving wrist extension. She reports some difficulty lifting objects due to pain but no issues with gripping. There is no swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, fever, chills, or systemic symptoms. She tried icing on the first day, which provided some relief. She denies any previous injuries or similar episodes in this elbow and reports no clicking, locking, or instability. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by wrist extension activities and a history of onset during tennis play. 

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; ensure dosing does not exceed recommended limits. Acetaminophen should be used within safe daily limits to avoid hepatotoxicity. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is new onset of numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow, or if systemic symptoms such as fever develop. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time based on history and symptomatology; consider imaging or specialist referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-8 weeks or worsen despite conservative management.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…
